spec_match.rationale: Uses anchor-name + position-anchor + @position-try (all real CSS) which the spec needs, but the v143 feature being demonstrated is @container anchored(fallback) — and the page never includes that syntax. The 'fallback badge' is computed in JS by measuring overflow, not by reading which @position-try fired or via a CSS container query.

interactive.rationale: Five anchor buttons, width/height sliders that live-resize the scroll container, fallback-badge toggle. Eight controls — real interactivity.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'five anchor buttons positioned at all edges and the center, each with a CSS-anchor-positioned tooltip using anchor-name, @position-try fallback blocks, and position-try-order' — page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: Comment in code admits 'We can't reliably read which @position-try is active via JS yet, so we infer from the computed position'. That inference (overflow vs not-overflow) is brittle — the badge will say 'preferred' even when a fallback fires, as long as the result still fits. The page also feature-detects anchor-name but doesn't separately probe the v143 anchored() container query.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only colour palette; no raw hex in the script body.

openQuestions.title: Add the @container anchored() rule that gates per-fallback styling
openQuestions.detail: The v143 feature is `@container anchored(fallback: --name)`. Pair each anchor button's tooltip with `container-type: anchored` and add CSS rules like `@container anchored(--top) { .tooltip { background: var(--accent-blue); } }`. Right now the page exercises @position-try (which has been in Chrome since 125) but not the v143 anchored-container-query syntax it's meant to demonstrate.

openQuestions.title: Replace the JS overflow-inference fallback badge
openQuestions.detail: The code's own comment admits the inference is approximate. Once the CSS container query is wired, the badge text can be driven by the same @container anchored() rule (e.g. `::after { content: 'fallback active' }`) — exact and CSS-only.

openQuestions.title: Add a CSS.supports probe for the v143 container-query syntax
openQuestions.detail: Probe `CSS.supports('container-type: anchored')` (or `CSS.supports('at-rule(@container)')` plus a runtime check) at the top of the page to tell visitors whether they're seeing the real v143 feature or the @position-try-only fallback. Right now the page only probes anchor-name (Chrome 125).

openQuestions.title: Show the position-try-order: most-block-size effect explicitly
openQuestions.detail: Blurb mentions `position-try-order: most-block-size` but the demo never highlights when that ordering changes the picked fallback. A toggle that compares with/without the order keyword on the same anchor would teach the ordering effect.

summary: Most CSS-driven of the four concepts — uses real @position-try fallbacks — but it never demonstrates the v143 feature itself (@container anchored()). The JS-inferred fallback badge is the clearest evidence: the page doesn't have access to the container-query feature it's supposedly showcasing.
